title: Amaretti, crisp
subtitle: The snap-textured Italian almond biscuit
excerpt: The harder, snap-textured amaretto, distinct from the soft chewy version. Ground almonds, sugar, and egg white baked at a higher temperature until the meringue crust hardens and the inside sets to a dry, brittle crumb. Naturally gluten-free.
sourceNotes: Amaretti are documented in Italian baking from at least the seventeenth century; the Saronno version (from which the liqueur takes its name) and the Sassello version represent the two classic Italian styles, soft and hard respectively. Pellegrino Artusi, La Scienza in Cucina e l'Arte di Mangiar Bene (1891), describes the dry amaretto as a traditional biscuit of northern Italy.

content.text: The crisp amaretto is the version you find wrapped in tissue in Italian tins: a thin, crackled shell and a dry, brittly interior. The

is essential; without it, they taste of macaroon rather than amaretto. Use a good-quality almond extract and do not undersell the quantity. The three-ingredient dough (ground almonds, sugar, egg white) comes together in a few minutes.

Preheat the oven to 180°C conventional (160°C fan) and line baking trays with baking paper or silicone mats. In a large bowl, mix the ground almonds and caster sugar together. Add the egg whites and almond extract and stir until a firm but workable paste forms. If it's too wet to handle, add a tablespoon more ground almonds.

Roll the paste into balls about 2 cm in diameter (roughly 12 g each). Roll each ball in icing sugar to coat generously, the icing sugar layer is what produces the characteristic cracked surface as the biscuit expands in the oven. Place 3 cm apart on the trays.

Bake for 18 to 22 minutes until the surface is cracked, the icing-sugar coating has set, and the biscuits feel firm when pressed. The base should be dry and pale gold. Cool completely on the tray before storing. They are fragile hot and firm up to a proper snap as they cool. Store in an airtight tin; moisture softens them.
